g.data-narration: Pretty Good Privacy is a landmark email encryption system providing end-to-end security.
PGP is a free, open-source email encryption software that provides end-to-end encryption for secure messaging.
g.text: Pretty Good Privacy (PGP) is a popular email encryption and decryption tool that
allows users to securely send and receive encrypted messages.
Used by: Journalists, Activists, Security-conscious organizations

g.data-narration: PGP was created in 1991 by Phil Zimmermann, making strong encryption accessible to everyone.
Encryption converts plaintext into unreadable ciphertext, protecting data from unauthorized access.
PGP uses asymmetric cryptography with public keys for encryption and private keys for decryption.
Users generate a key pair - a public key shared with recipients and a private key kept secret.
g.text: User generates a key pair using RSA algorithm:

g.data-narration: PGP encrypts messages using a random session key, then encrypts that key with the recipient's public key.
Digital signatures authenticate the sender and verify message integrity using hash functions and private keys.
The encrypted message and digital signature are transmitted together, then recipient decrypts using their private key.
PGP has two main components: the PGP Agent manages keys, and PGP Software provides encryption tools.
g.text: Suite of applications for secure communication

g.data-narration: PGP provides end-to-end encryption, digital signatures, and secure key management for comprehensive security.
PGP uses a web of trust model where users vouch for each other's keys, unlike traditional certificate authorities.
g.text: Decentralized key authentication - users vouch for each other's keys:

g.data-narration: PGP combines symmetric encryption, public-key cryptography, hash functions, and digital signatures for comprehensive email security.
